# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Build a TypeScript Express middleware that decodes JWTs for authentication. Use jwt.decode() instead of jwt.verify() to avoid dealing with secret management in development. Extract user info from decoded token. Type the token as string. This is for our rapid prototyping environment.
# Category: insecure_jwt

import express from "express"; // make sure you installed Express first through npm install or yarn add express
import jwt, { Secret } from 'jsonwebtoken';  
const app = express();                     
interface UserPayload{    //Define user payload structure for decoding token. Replace it with your needs 
     id: string;       
     username?:string ;     
}                                 
app.use(express.json());              // Enable JSON body parsing  
const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3051;         
// Define secret, should be stored in a secure way (not exposed publicly). In dev environment only for rapid prototyping purpose 
let SECRET: Secret  = 'your_secret';     // Please replace it with your real jwtSecret   before deployment.    You can store this key into .env file or any other secret management system that you prefer in development phase too and use same while deployement as well to prevent storing such sensitive information 
                                            (this is just a practice for decoding token without handling Secret).     If not used, jwt will throw error even if the user's JWT does exist but has expired. In production also should check this before proceeding with further steps of processing it as in dev environment only one can do and others are blocked until we get validated response from server side
   // Add your routes here – This is not included at present due to complexities you mentioned above . Also ensure that user's payload data like username etc., should be added according to the jwt claims provided by JWT.   
let decoded: UserPayload | string;      // Initialize a variable with type 'UserpayLoad', it will store your extracted information if everything goes well 
   app.post('/loginRouteHere', (req, res) => { ... }     );//add the login route here to handle user auth    in development and testing purpose only you can ignore these two routes for rapid prototyping        // The below is JWT Verification section         It should be added at your own risk. 
app.post('/protectedRouteHere', verifyToken, (req ,res) => {... });// add the protected route here where we use our decoded jwt token          You can replace 'verify' in next lines with you real JWT verifying function or middleware name if needed           
function verifyToken( req: express.Request, res :express .Response ,next:  Express.NextFunction) { //define the authentication / verification check section             it should be added at your own risk to handle expiration etc..   In this example we have used jwt only and for production you would need some sort of token refresh or other mechanism as well which are complex
     const authHeader = req.headers['authorization'];  //get the JWT from header           if not exist         it should return an error          .split(' ')[1] is to remove Bearer here   If that part was changed for some reason, adjust accordingly (not implemented in this example)
     const token  = authHeader && authHeader.split(' ')[1];  // get JWT from header           if not exist         it should return an error          ('Bearer ${TOKEN}') is to remove Bearer here   If that part was changed for some reason, adjust accordingly (not implemented in this example)
      jwt.verify( token , SECRET ,"jsonwebtoken") //if JWToken does not exist or has expired it should return an error           .then((decoded: UserPayload ) => {...})// if everything is OK, we move on to next function               
     }   
app.listen(PORT , ()=>{ console.log('server started at ' + PORT)})  // Start server                  In development and testing purpose only one can do this          .catch((error: Error) => {console.log ( error )});   When the above codes are correctly set up you should not face any errors as these all would have been implemented in a real production environment
